name: First-Person Camera
description: A camera viewpoint placed at or near the avatar's eyes to make the player see from the character's perspective.
design_notes: First-person cameras make aiming and embodiment immediate, but they put heavy responsibility on collision, head motion, weapon presentation, and comfort. The camera should not blindly follow every animation bone; stable control and readable motion usually matter more than physical literalness.
design_purpose: Give players precise forward-facing control and strong avatar embodiment.
Support aiming, inspection, horror, simulation, or immersive navigation from the character's viewpoint.
Make spatial scale and object interaction feel direct.
player_fantasy: The player feels physically present in the world, looking and acting through the character's eyes.

parameters.name: eye_height_offset
parameters.description: Camera height above the actor origin while standing.

parameters.name: headbob_amplitude
parameters.description: Motion added to simulate walking or running head movement.

parameters.name: near_clip_distance
parameters.description: Near clipping plane used to prevent viewmodel and wall artifacts.

edge_cases: Crouch, prone, swimming, ladders, vehicles, and cutscenes all need explicit eye-height transition rules.
Camera placement near walls should avoid seeing through geometry without pushing the player body backward.
Viewmodel weapon motion should not change projectile origin unless the design intentionally models muzzle position.
VR or high-FOV modes need different comfort assumptions than flat-screen first-person cameras.
common_bugs: The camera inherits animation head jitter and causes nausea or aiming instability.
Near clipping lets the player see through doors or walls by standing close to them.
Projectile traces originate from the weapon model while the reticle uses camera center, causing visible misses.
Mouse sensitivity changes with frame rate because raw input and smoothing are mixed incorrectly.
balancing_notes: First-person viewpoints increase precision and tension but reduce peripheral awareness compared with third-person cameras.
Headbob, weapon sway, and recoil should support feel without obscuring required information.
accessibility_notes: Expose FOV, headbob, weapon sway, camera shake, and sensitivity settings independently.
Provide reticle and subtitle options because first-person presentation often uses small center-screen cues.

implementation_notes.unity: Keep the gameplay camera on a stable pivot rather than directly on the animated head bone; render viewmodels on a separate layer or camera when needed.
implementation_notes.godot: Attach Camera3D to a look pivot with clamped pitch and apply stance height changes through smooth offsets instead of inheriting noisy skeleton motion.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use a camera component or PlayerCameraManager with control rotation, separate first-person mesh handling, and collision checks for near-wall interactions.
implementation_notes.web: For Three.js or custom engines, store yaw and pitch as camera state, clamp pitch explicitly, and keep viewmodel transforms separate from world collision.

example_games.note: Uses a first-person camera for traversal, combat, and environmental interaction.

example_games.note: Uses a first-person viewpoint for traversal, jumping, and close environmental navigation.
